465	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
466	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
467	
468	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
469	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
470	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
471	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
472	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
473	
474	\begin{definition}\leanok
475	[Global section of the unit module]
476	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
478	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
479	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
480	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
481	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
482	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
483	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
484	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
485	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
486	  global function.
487	\end{definition}
488	
489	\begin{definition}\leanok
490	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
491	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
493	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
494	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
495	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
496	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
497	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
498	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
499	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
500	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
501	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
502	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
503	\end{definition}
504	
505	\begin{lemma}\leanok
506	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
507	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
509	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
510	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
511	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
512	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
513	\end{lemma}
514	
515	\begin{proof}
516	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
517	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
518	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
519	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{lemma}\leanok
523	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
524	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
526	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
527	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
528	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
529	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
534	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
535	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}\leanok
539	[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
540	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
541	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
542	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
543	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
544	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
545	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
546	\end{lemma}
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
549	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
550	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
551	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
552	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
553	\end{proof}
554	
555	\begin{lemma}\leanok
556	[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
557	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
559	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
560	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
561	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
562	\end{lemma}
563	\begin{proof}
564	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
565	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
566	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
567	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
568	\end{proof}

570	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
571	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
572	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
573	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
574	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
575	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
576	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
577	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
578	
579	\begin{definition}\leanok
580	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
581	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
582	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
583	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
584	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
585	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
586	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
587	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
588	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
589	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
590	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
591	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
592	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
593	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
594	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
595	\end{definition}
596	
597	\begin{lemma}\leanok
598	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
599	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
600	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
601	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
602	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
603	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
604	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
605	  \[
606	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
607	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
608	  \]
609	\end{lemma}
610	\begin{proof}
611	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
612	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
613	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
614	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
615	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
616	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
617	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
618	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
619	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
620	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
621	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
622	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
623	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\begin{lemma}\leanok
627	[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
628	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
630	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
631	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
632	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
633	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
634	\end{lemma}
635	\begin{proof}
636	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
637	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
638	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
639	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
640	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
641	\end{proof}

643	\begin{definition}\leanok
644	[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
645	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
647	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
648	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
649	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
650	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
651	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
652	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
653	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
654	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
655	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
656	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
657	\end{definition}
658	
659	\begin{lemma}\leanok
660	[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
661	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
662	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
663	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
664	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
665	\end{lemma}
666	\begin{proof}
667	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
668	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
669	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
670	\end{proof}

672	\begin{lemma}[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively]
673	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
674	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
675	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
676	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
677	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
678	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
679	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
680	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
681	  \[
682	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
683	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
684	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
685	  \]
686	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
687	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
688	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
689	\end{lemma}
690	\begin{proof}
691	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
692	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
693	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
694	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
695	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.
696	\end{proof}

698	\begin{definition}
699	
700	\leanok
701	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
702	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
703	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
704	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
705	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
706	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
707	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
708	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
709	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
710	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
711	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
712	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
713	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
714	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
715	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
716	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
717	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
718	  \[
719	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
720	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
721	  \]
722	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
723	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
724	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
725	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
726	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
727	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
728	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
729	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
730	
731	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
732	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
733	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
734	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
735	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
736	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
737	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
738	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
739	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
740	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
741	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
742	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
743	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
744	  free sheaves'' primitive.
745	\end{definition}

747	\begin{lemma}\leanok
748	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
749	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
751	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
752	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
753	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
754	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
755	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
756	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
757	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
758	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
759	  identity:
760	  \[
761	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
762	  \]
763	\end{lemma}
764	
765	\begin{proof}
766	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
767	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
768	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
769	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
770	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
771	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
772	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
773	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
774	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
775	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
776	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
777	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
778	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
779	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
780	\end{proof}

782	\begin{lemma}\leanok
783	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
784	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
785	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
786	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
787	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
788	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
789	\end{lemma}
790	
791	\begin{proof}
792	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
793	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
794	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
795	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
796	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
797	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
798	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
799	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
800	  \[
801	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
802	  \]
803	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
804	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
805	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
806	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
807	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
808	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
809	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
810	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
811	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
812	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
813	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
814	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
815	\end{proof}

817	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
818	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
819	
820	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
821	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
822	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
823	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
824	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
825	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
826	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
827	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
828	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
829	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
830	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
831	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.
832	
833	\begin{definition}\leanok
834	[The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\)]
835	  \label{def:gr_bundleTransition}
836	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition}
837	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
838	    lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition}
839	  Let \(I, J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) be size-\(d\) subsets and let
840	  \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) and \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be
841	  the overlap immersion and transition of the chart cover (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The
842	  \emph{bundle transition} \(g_{I,J}\) is the isomorphism of sheaves of modules on the
843	  overlap \(U^I_J\)
844	  \[
845	    g_{I,J} \;:\;
846	      f_{IJ}^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\bigr)
847	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
848	      (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^J}^{\,d}\bigr)
849	  \]
850	  induced by the invertible \(d \times d\) matrix
851	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J)\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
852	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor, which is invertible because

1119	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(r,d)` (`def:grassmannian_functor`): the
1120	contravariant functor from schemes to sets sending `T` to the set of equivalence classes
1121	of rank-`d` locally free quotients `q : O_T^r ↠ F`, acting on morphisms by pullback.
1122	
1123	The object and morphism assignments are complete; the functoriality laws (`map_id`,
1124	`map_comp`) are discharged — via the naturality of the pseudofunctor comparison isomorphisms
1125	`pullbackId`/`pullbackComp` of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` — through the free-sheaf coherences
1126	`pullbackFreeIso_id`/`pullbackFreeIso_comp`, which reduce by coproduct extensionality to the
1127	unit-level coherences `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id`/`pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp`. Fully proved. -/
1128	noncomputable def functor (d r : ℕ) : Scheme.{0}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type 1 where
1129	  obj T := Quotient (rqSetoid r d T.unop)
1130	  map {X Y} g := TypeCat.ofHom (Quotient.map (rqPullback (r := r) (d := d) g.unop)
1131	    (fun _ _ h => rqPullback_rel g.unop h))
1132	  map_id X := by
1133	    -- reduce to the equivalence relation on representatives
1134	    ext z
1135	    induction z using Quotient.ind with
1136	    | _ x =>
1137	      change Quotient.mk _ (rqPullback (𝟙 X).unop x) = Quotient.mk _ x
1138	      -- the canonical iso `(𝟙)^* x.F ≅ x.F` is `pullbackId`; the quotient-map equation it
1139	      -- must satisfy reduces, by naturality of `pullbackId.hom`, to the free coherence
1140	      -- `pullbackFreeIso (𝟙) = (pullbackId).app (free _)`, hence to the unit-level identity
1141	      -- `pullbackObjUnitToUnit (𝟙) = (pullbackId).app unit`. That coherence between
1142	      -- `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` and Mathlib's `pullbackId` is the open obstacle.
1143	      refine Quotient.sound ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullbackId X.unop).app x.F, ?_⟩
1144	      -- unfold `(rqPullback (𝟙) x).q` and `(pullbackId.app x.F).hom` (defeq)
1145	      change ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso (𝟙 X.unop) (Fin r)).inv ≫
1146	          (Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 X.unop)).map x.q) ≫
1147	          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId X.unop).hom.app x.F = x.q
1148	      rw [Category.assoc, (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId X.unop).hom.naturality x.q,
1149	        ← Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id]
1150	      -- `(𝟭).map x.q = x.q` is only defeq, so close by term (rw can't see through it)
1151	      exact Iso.inv_hom_id_assoc _ _
1152	  map_comp {X Y Z} f g := by
1153	    ext z
1154	    induction z using Quotient.ind with
1155	    | _ x =>
1156	      change Quotient.mk _ (rqPullback (f ≫ g).unop x)
1157	        = Quotient.mk _ (rqPullback g.unop (rqPullback f.unop x))
1158	      -- the canonical iso `(g.unop ≫ f.unop)^* x.F ≅ g.unop^*(f.unop^* x.F)` is `pullbackComp`;
1159	      -- the quotient-map equation reduces, by naturality, to the composite free coherence
1160	      -- relating `pullbackFreeIso (g.unop ≫ f.unop)` to `pullbackFreeIso g.unop`/`f.unop`
1161	      -- through `pullbackComp` — the composite analogue of the `map_id` obstacle.
1162	      refine Quotient.sound ⟨((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).app x.F).symm, ?_⟩
1163	      -- unfold `(rqPullback (g∘f) x).q` and `(pullbackComp.app x.F).symm.hom` (defeq), writing
1164	      -- the composite as `g.unop ≫ f.unop` so the `pullbackComp` naturality matches syntactically
1165	      change ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso (g.unop ≫ f.unop) (Fin r)).inv ≫
1166	          (Scheme.Modules.pullback (g.unop ≫ f.unop)).map x.q) ≫
1167	          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).inv.app x.F
1168	        = (rqPullback g.unop (rqPullback f.unop x)).q
1169	      -- expose the `pullbackComp.inv` naturality square (mirrors the `map_id` reduction)
1170	      rw [Category.assoc, (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).inv.naturality x.q]
1171	      -- the composite free coherence (`pullbackFreeIso_comp`) in inverse form: invert both
1172	      -- sides of the iso equation `pullbackComp.hom.app free ≫ pfba.hom = (pullback g).map pfa.hom
1173	      -- ≫ pfb.hom`.
1174	      have hstar : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso (g.unop ≫ f.unop) (Fin r)).inv ≫
1175	            (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).inv.app (SheafOfModules.free (Fin r))
1176	          = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso g.unop (Fin r)).inv ≫
1177	            (Scheme.Modules.pullback g.unop).map
1178	              (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso f.unop (Fin r)).inv := by
1179	        have hH := Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp f.unop g.unop (Fin r)
1180	        rw [← cancel_epi ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).hom.app
1181	          (SheafOfModules.free (Fin r)) ≫
1182	          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso (g.unop ≫ f.unop) (Fin r)).hom)]
1183	        trans (𝟙 _)
1184	        · rw [Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc]
1185	          exact (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g.unop f.unop).hom_inv_id_app _
1186	        · rw [hH]; simp
1187	      -- whisker `hstar` by `≫ (pullback f ⋙ pullback g).map x.q` and refold the RHS via
1188	      -- `map_comp` into `(rqPullback g (rqPullback f x)).q`.
1189	      exact (Category.assoc _ _ _).symm.trans
1190	        ((congrArg (· ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback f.unop ⋙
1191	              Scheme.Modules.pullback g.unop).map x.q) hstar).trans
1192	          ((Category.assoc _ _ _).trans
1193	            (congrArg ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso g.unop (Fin r)).inv ≫ ·)
1194	              ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.unop).map_comp
1195	                (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso f.unop (Fin r)).inv
1196	                ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f.unop).map x.q)).symm)))

1062	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1063	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1064	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1065	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1066	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1067	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1068	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,

1070	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1071	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1072	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1073	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1074	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1075	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1076	  \[
1077	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1078	  \]
1079	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1080	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
1081	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1082	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1083	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1084	  \[
1085	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1086	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1087	  \]
1088	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1089	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1090	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1091	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1092	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1093	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1094	\end{definition}

1096	\section{The functor of points}
1097	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1098	
1099	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1100	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1101	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1102	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1103	identities that make that action functorial.
1104	
1105	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1106	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1108	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1109	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1110	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1111	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1112	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1113	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1114	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1115	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1116	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1117	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1118	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1119	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1120	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1121	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1122	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1123	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1124	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1125	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1126	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1127	  functor's value at \(T\).
1128	
1129	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1130	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1131	  \[
1132	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1133	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1134	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1135	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1136	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1137	  \]
1138	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1139	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1140	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1141	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1142	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1143	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1144	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1145	  map of equivalence classes.
1146	\end{definition}
1147	
1148	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1149	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1150	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1151	
1152	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1153	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1154	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1156	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1157	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1158	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1159	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1160	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1161	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1162	\end{lemma}
1163	
1164	\begin{proof}
1165	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1166	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1167	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1168	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1169	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1170	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1171	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1172	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1173	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1174	\end{proof}
1175	
1176	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1177	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1178	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1180	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1181	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1182	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1183	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1184	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1185	\end{lemma}
1186	
1187	\begin{proof}
1188	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1189	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1190	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1191	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1192	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1193	\end{proof}
1194	
1195	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1196	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1197	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1199	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1200	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1201	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1202	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1203	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1204	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
1205	\end{lemma}
1206	
1207	\begin{proof}
1208	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1209	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1210	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1211	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1212	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1213	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1214	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1215	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1216	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1217	  well-defined.
1218	\end{proof}
1219	
1220	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1221	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1222	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1223	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1224	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1225	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1226	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1227	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1228	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1229	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1230	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1231	\end{lemma}
1232	
1233	\begin{proof}
1234	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1235	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1236	  hom-set bijections.
1237	\end{proof}
1238	
1239	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1240	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1241	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1243	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1244	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1245	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1246	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1247	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1248	\end{lemma}
1249	
1250	\begin{proof}
1251	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1252	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1253	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element

1255	  tracked through each pullback).

1258	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1259	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1260	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1261	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1262	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1263	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1264	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.

1266	\begin{definition}
1267	
1268	\leanok
1269	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1270	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1272	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1273	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1274	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1275	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1276	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1277	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1278	  % "represents the
1279	  %  contravariant functor
1280	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1281	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1282	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1283	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1284	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1285	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1286	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1287	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1288	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1289	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1290	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1291	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1292	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1293	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1294	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1295	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1296	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1297	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1298	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1299	  \[
1300	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1301	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1302	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1303	  \]
1304	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1305	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1306	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1307	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1308	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1309	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1310	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1311	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1312	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1313	\end{definition}

1315	\section{The universal property}
1316	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1317	
1318	\begin{theorem}
1319	
1320	\leanok
1321	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1322	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1323	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1324	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1325	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1326	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1327	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1328	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1329	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1330	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1331	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1332	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1333	  %  contravariant functor
1334	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1335	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1336	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1337	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1338	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1339	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1340	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1341	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1342	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1343	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1344	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1345	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1346	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1347	  \[
1348	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1349	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1350	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1351	    \qquad
1352	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1353	      \;\longmapsto\;
1354	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1355	  \]
1356	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1357	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1358	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1359	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1360	  universal family.
1361	\end{theorem}
1362	
1363	\begin{proof}
1364	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1365	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1366	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1367	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1368	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1369	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1370	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1371	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1372	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1373	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1374	
1375	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1376	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1377	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1378	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1379	  the composite
1380	  \[
1381	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1382	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1383	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1384	  \]
1385	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1386	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1387	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1388	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1389	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1390	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1391	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1392	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1393	
1394	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1395	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1396	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1397	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1398	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1399	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1400	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1401	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1402	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1403	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1404	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1405	
1406	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1407	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1408	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1409	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1410	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1411	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1412	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1413	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
